title: How To Move A Prospect To A Client
summary: Erin walked participants through her four-pillar framework for converting prospects into clients, covering consistent social media presence, building strategic feeder relationships, continuous competence development, and self-awareness of personal strengths. The session opened into group Q&A where participants discussed contract structuring, management fee models, qualifying clients, handling difficult feeders, and rebuilding pipelines in slow markets.

teachings.concept: The Four Pillars for Moving a Prospect to a Client
teachings.explanation: Erin's framework holds that converting prospects happens before any formal meeting through four non-negotiable daily practices: showing up on social media, building feeder relationships, continuously building competence, and knowing what you are personally known for. All of this work happens when nobody is watching.
teachings.quote: All of moving a prospect to a client happens when nobody's watching, like nobody's paying attention to all the stuff you're doing and all the things you're aligning.

teachings.concept: Consistent Social Media Presence as Pre-Selling
teachings.explanation: Posting daily on social media allows potential clients to feel like they already know you before a formal introduction. This speeds up the qualification process because people either self-select in or out based on what you share, eliminating misaligned prospects before they consume your time.
teachings.quote: People feel most comfortable when they feel like they know something about you even though they've never met you.

teachings.explanation: Feeders are architects, designers, vendors, and trades whose client profiles align with the type of work you want. Rather than blasting outreach to hundreds of contacts, identify the top five relationships that bridge where you are to where you want to be and invest in serving them first.
teachings.quote: Don't ever discount trades and companies you work with and vendors. Some of the best projects we've been involved in were from our home automation company.

teachings.explanation: Competence is not a function of time or seniority; it requires deliberate daily learning. One of the fastest methods is to ask questions immediately after every meeting with architects, engineers, or specialists, converting each interaction into a learning opportunity that builds the knowledge bank you draw on when talking to future clients.
teachings.quote: Every meeting I have a follow-up with whatever I learned during that meeting every day. That's one way to do it. That speeds it up fast.

teachings.explanation: Regularly analyzing what you are genuinely known for — whether technical visualization, communication, patience, or follow-through — gives you a clearer message to deliver to prospects and helps clients self-select based on an accurate picture of how you operate, preventing misaligned expectations later.
teachings.quote: It's important to really analyze at least weekly what you're known for, because when you get a better understanding of what you're really good at, it becomes clearer to deliver that message for future clients.

teachings.explanation: Regularly playing out hypothetical client and project scenarios in your mind — from upset clients to scheduling conflicts — builds the mental muscle to respond calmly and confidently when real situations arise. The comfort you feel in the moment comes from having already processed the scenario.
teachings.quote: The more scenarios and routes you're running in your head, the better you can address anything that comes up and feel comfortable doing it. That's the catch — feel comfortable, because you've already played these stories out.

teachings.explanation: When a client requests a fixed management fee structure, there is a financial breaking point around five million dollars in project cost. Below that threshold a fixed fee can work well; above it, general overhead requirements tend to erode profitability. The fee should be calculated by summing project manager costs plus overhead plus profit, then dividing by the projected duration with buffer months added.
teachings.quote: A fixed management fee for anything under five million dollars is a good thing. Anything over five million dollars you tend to lose money based on what your general overhead requirements are.

teachings.explanation: Rather than concealing how you arrive at your numbers or contract terms, openly sharing your methodology with sophisticated clients — including contract attorneys — builds trust and demonstrates professionalism. Asking what the client is most uncomfortable about surfaces objections early and creates space for honest resolution.
teachings.quote: I share the methodology. I literally don't hold back. With clients I just say here's my methodology, here's how I arrived at this number. What do you think?

teachings.concept: Be Open to Everything, Attached to Nothing
teachings.explanation: When facing financial pressure and unsure whether a prospect will convert, approaching conversations without attachment to the outcome paradoxically makes them easier and more genuine. This mindset, attributed to Wayne Dyer, reduces desperation energy and allows the right fit to emerge naturally.
teachings.quote: Be open to everything but attached to nothing. When I had this conversation I told myself I'm not attached to the outcome here, I'm just not. And then it was like easy.

actionable_advice: Post at least once a day on social media and share three stories a day consistently, without judging the content — just get it out there to let people know who you are before they meet you.

Identify the top five architects or designers who work with the client profile you want, then find two trades who already work with each of those professionals and ask them to set up a lunch introduction.
After every meeting with an architect, engineer, or specialist, ask if you can use ten minutes of their drive time to ask questions about things you did not understand in that meeting.

When structuring a management fee for a project, calculate project manager costs at your monthly rate multiplied by the estimated project duration plus three buffer months, add overhead and profit, then divide by total months to arrive at a monthly fee.

Include a weekly reporting requirement in your contracts and explicitly link timeline changes to contract adjustments so that delays caused by client decisions trigger a formal conversation about terms.
When a client requests a fixed management fee, build the full budget transparently behind the scenes using your standard methodology, then share the methodology openly and ask for their feedback.

When building feeders, look beyond the obvious — consider photographers who shoot high-end homes, luxury magazine events, or art auctions where target clients and design professionals gather, and pay for a spot if needed.

Practice difficult client or project scenarios with your project manager before real meetings — have them present to you while you challenge them aggressively so the team is prepared to respond calmly under pressure.

When a feeder sends a referral without following your intake process, gently redirect by asking for the client's contact information so you can set up your initial call and walk them through your process rather than going directly on-site.

Pick two or three specific technical details from a prospect's plans that illustrate the unique complexity of their home, and use those examples in early meetings to demonstrate your competency and help them understand why their project will take longer than production building.
client_challenges: Ryan is working to convert a high-attention, detail-oriented prospect whose husband is a contract attorney and wants a management fee structure, and is unsure how to price the fee, handle potential timeline overruns, and differentiate himself from the competing builder she is also considering.

Sandy is finishing current projects with no signed next project, has intentionally turned down work that does not align with her target direction, and is wrestling with whether to take misaligned projects for cash flow or hold out for the right fit.

David is rebuilding his pipeline in the Austin market after the real estate market collapsed and his previous feeder base of real estate agents dried up, compounded by losses on past spec homes and a difficult project that resulted in costly litigation.

David is also struggling to identify criteria for filtering out difficult prospects early, particularly clients who raise litigation language or insurance demands in initial meetings, after a prior experience that cost over one hundred thousand dollars in legal fees.

Renee is having difficulty getting feeder designers to respect her intake process — feeders are sending referrals directly expecting an on-site visit without the client first going through Erin's standard filtering steps.
Janine asked whether Erin has ever had a dedicated sales or business development person beyond herself, raising the question of how and when to delegate the early-stage client relationship role.
A participant shared being in a slow market cycle and questioned whether to take on less-than-ideal projects to maintain cash flow or stay the course building feeders and waiting for the right work.
notable_quotes: On pre-selling through consistency: 'All of moving a prospect to a client happens when nobody's watching, like nobody's paying attention to all the stuff you're doing and all the things you're aligning.'

On making promises to clients: 'When we first met there's a promise you made to us and there's a promise we made to you and no matter what's going on in your life we're going to deliver on that promise. Period.'

On mindset when facing scarcity: 'Be open to everything but attached to nothing. When I had this conversation I told myself I'm not attached to the outcome here, I'm just not. And then it was like easy.'

On what clients are actually buying: 'You have reduced their risk. That's what they want. The builder fails to show up the last six months of the house — it is all over the country. You're structuring their risk.'

On the white team analogy for character in business: 'The white team will never play a game but they show up every single day and they do their work, and when it's scrimmage time that white team tries to crush the red team and nobody cares about them. That's character. That's a lot of character. And so that's what's happening in our businesses.'

On desperation as a motivator: 'If I had zero money right now and I have five kids I have to feed them today, what would I go do? I would call all of my clients and say hey, is anybody handling your homeowner maintenance? That's what I would do.'
